segments.s534-g33: نشست is a past 3rd‑person singular verb meaning “sat” or “took a seat.” اندران is a locative prepositional adverb “among them/amongst” that follows the verb in this elevated/poetic word order; پاک فربه بره is the noun phrase ‘the plump, clean lamb’ (پاک and فربه are descriptive adjectives modifying بره). The whole clause therefore reads literally “sat among them (a) pure, fat lamb.”
segments.s534-g34: که introduces a subordinate/result clause (“such that/where”), pointing back to the lamb. تیر is “arrow.” اندرو is a pronominal locative “in it/in him,” referring to the lamb. غرقه شد is a two‑word predication where غرقه (immersed/drenched) plus شد (became) describes a resulting state: “became immersed/was sunk.” یکسره is an adverb “completely.” Together: “so that the arrow became completely immersed in it.”
segments.s534-g35: This is a noun cluster naming the people: بزرگان “the great/important ones” (plural), فرزانه “wise/sage” and رزمساز a compound/title meaning ‘war‑maker’ or ‘warrior/commander.’ The three words stand together as an appositive/epithet describing the actors of the following clause.
segments.s534-g36: ز نان here is a causal/prepositional phrase “from/because of bread” (poetic ز = از). داشتند is the past plural of داشتن “they had.” آن زمان is a temporal phrase “at that time/then.” دست باز is an idiom: دست “hand” + باز “free/available,” meaning ‘a free hand’ or ‘not constrained / having enough resources.’ The sequence means roughly “because of bread they had (then) a free hand” — i.e. they were not lacking bread.
segments.s534-g37: بدیدند is past plural “they saw.” نقشی here means an inscription/engraving or carved design (نقش used for a mark or written/figural impression). بران is a prepositional/locative compound meaning “upon that/thereon,” and تیز تیر is the noun phrase “the sharp arrow.” The clause: “They saw an inscription upon that sharp arrow.”
segments.s534-g38: بخواند is classical/persian past 3rd‑person (b‑ + خواند) meaning “(he) read” or “it was read.” آنک functions as a relative/pronominal particle “the one who / which.” بد here is an archaic literary form of بود (was) rather than the adjective “bad” (an archaic contraction common in epic Persian), so the short clause should be parsed as “the one who was among those great men a دبیر (scribe) read it.” Thus the sequence means “it was read by the scribe who was one of those great men.”
segments.s534-g39: ز غم is a causal phrase “because of/from sorrow.” هرکسی here means “everyone / each person.” از جگر خون کشید is an idiomatic violent expression: از جگر “from the heart/liver” (jegar as the seat of feeling) + خون کشید “drew blood” (i.e. cut themselves or bled in lament). کشید is the past tense of کشیدن “to draw/pull,” so the clause says everyone “drew blood from his heart” from grief.
segments.s534-g40: یکی “one (of them / someone)” + از بره “from the lamb” indicates the agent and source: someone drew (pulled) something out of the lamb. تیر is “the arrow.” بیرون is the adverb “out,” and کشید is again past of کشیدن “he pulled/drew.” So: “one pulled the arrow out from the lamb.”
segments.s534-g1: به جهرم = locative prepositional phrase placing the scene in/at Jahrom; یکی مرد = 'a certain man' (one + noun), with یکی before the noun as an indefinite marker. بد بدنژاد is a compound adjective (بد + بدنژاد) meaning 'ill/low‑born' modifying مرد. Word order is typical Persian: place → (indefinite) subject → adjectival description.
segments.s534-g2: کجا here functions as an archaic/poetic relative particle introducing the following apposition (literally 'whose'); نام او مهرک نوش‌زاد is a verbless nominal clause meaning 'his name (was) Mehrak Nush‑zād' with the copula omitted. مهرک نوش‌زاد is a personal name plus epithet.
segments.s534-g3: چو + predicate = temporal clause 'when...'. آگه شد is a past‑tense verbal phrase 'became aware/was informed'; از + verbal noun رفتن marks the content/thing learned ('of the going/departure of Ardashir'). The clause ends with the name اردشیر as the object of از رفتن.
segments.s534-g4: وزان is a connective/adverb ('and/from that time') linking the result; ماندن او here functions as the verb phrase 'he stayed/remained' (ماندن = remain/stay, with the pronoun او marking the agent). بران آبگیر = 'at that pond/ reservoir' where بران is a demonstrative/adverbial ('thereon/thence') and آبگیر names the location. Overall: 'and from that he remained at that pond.'
segments.s534-g5: ز تنگی = 'because of the narrowness' (ز used causatively). که introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing that narrowness; بد اندر آن رزمگاه is a verbless clause meaning 'was bad (or dire) in that battlefield' with اندر = 'in/within' and آن رزمگاه = 'that battlefield.' The copula is omitted in the relative clause, typical of classical poetic style.
segments.s534-g6: ز بهر خورشها = 'for the sake/for the purpose of the stews' (بهر indicating purpose/benefit and خورشها the plural 'stews/food'). برو بسته راه = a terse, poetic word order meaning 'the way was closed off for bringing (them)' — بسته modifies راه ('closed road/route'), and برو here is an old/poetic particle tied to the action of bringing/going (it functions adverbially/relationally in this clause).
segments.s534-g7: ز جهرم بیامد = 'he came from Jahrom' with ز marking origin and بیامد the simple past 'came'. به ایوان شاه is the destination phrase 'to the king's iwan' (به + noun + genitive noun), standard Persian order: verb then prepositional destination.
segments.s534-g8: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (adverbial). بیاورد is a narrative/past form of آوردن meaning 'he brought' or 'would bring' (habitual/iterative sense in narrative). بی‌مر is an archaic adjective modifying سپاه; its exact nuance is uncertain in modern Persian but functions here to intensify/qualify سپاه (an army/host). Word order places the adjective before the noun.
segments.s534-g9: همه modifies گنج to mean 'the whole/entire treasure.' گنج او را is the direct object (گنج + possessive pronoun او + object marker را), and به تاراج داد is a light‑verb construction: به تاراج (pillage) + دادن (داد) = 'to plunder' or literally 'gave to pillage.' The past verb داد has an implicit subject (the plunderers) typical of narrative Persian.
segments.s534-g10: به لشکر marks the beneficiary/recipient ('to the army'). بسی here is an adverbial intensifier ('many/ample') modifying the following noun بدره. بدره و تاج form a coordinate object ('many moon‑like beauties and crowns') and داد (again) is the past tense verb 'gave.' Note the poetic ordering: objects and modifiers precede the verb.
segments.s534-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' آگاهی آمد is an impersonal/neutral idiom 'news/information came' (آگاهی as the thing that arrives). به شاه اردشیر marks the recipient with به ('to King Ardashir'). Word order (چو + NP + آمد + به NP) is standard for reporting an incoming report.
segments.s534-g12: پراندیشه is an adjectival predicate 'pensive/thoughtful'; شد is the copular/auxiliary past 'became.' بر لب آبگیر is a locative prepositional phrase: بر ('on/at') + لب ('brink/edge') + آبگیر ('pond/reservoir'), giving 'became pensive at the edge of the reservoir.'
segments.s534-g13: همی + گفت forms a durative past: همی gives the sense 'kept on / continuously' and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here reported speech). ناساخته is an adjective 'not-built/unfinished' placed before the noun (poetic inversion), and خانه را (house + object marker را) is the definite thing he speaks about.
segments.s534-g14: چرا is the interrogative 'why' introducing the quoted question. ساختم is first‑person past 'I built/caused'; رزم بیگانه را is the direct object 'the battle/war of (or with) strangers' (رزم 'battle' modified by the adjective بیگانه), with را marking it definite. The verb comes at the end, reflecting Persian SOV order within the quotation.
segments.s534-g15: بزرگان لشکرش را is a noun phrase 'the great ones/chiefs of his army' (لشکرش = 'his army') marked with را as the object. پیش here means 'before (him)/in his presence,' and خواند (from خواندن) is used in the sense 'to call/summon'—so the clause means 'he summoned the chiefs of his army before him.'
segments.s534-g16: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by.' مهرک here functions as a proper name (Mehrak) as source/agent of what follows. فراوان modifies سخنها ('many/abundant words'), and براند (from راندن 'to drive') is used metaphorically 'to drive forth/utter'—so literally 'from Mehrak he drove forth many words,' i.e. Mehrak (or by means of Mehrak) uttered many speeches.
segments.s534-g17: This is direct speech followed by narration: 'چه بینید' is a present second‑person plural interrogative ('what do you see?') addressed to the commanders; 'گفت' is the past narrative verb 'he said' that attaches to the whole quoted question. 'ای سران سپاه' is a vocative phrase ('O chiefs of the army') placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s534-g18: This subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that') gives the content/reason: 'ما را' uses the object/emphatic marker 'را' with 'ما' (us/our). 'چنین تنگ شد دستگاه' uses 'شد' as the past of 'to become' ('became'), so literally 'the dastāgāh (court/state) became so narrow/strained' — i.e. our situation was made straitened.
segments.s534-g19: 'چشیدم' is first‑person past ('I tasted' / 'I experienced'). 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'very many', and it modifies 'تلخی روزگار' ('the bitterness of fate/times'). Word order places the verb first (typical of narrative poetic Persian) followed by the quantified object.
segments.s534-g20: 'نبد' here is an older/poetic negative existential ('was not' / 'did not exist'). The phrase 'رنج مهرک مرا در شمار' literally 'the hardship of Mehrak (رنج مهرک) — for me (مرا) — in the count/list (در شمار)' means 'the suffering from Mehrak was not counted among (my hardships)'. Note that 'م' + 'را' shows the object marker attached to the pronoun; the line uses an archaic split tokenization.
segments.s534-g21: 'به آواز' is an adverbial phrase ('with voice' = 'aloud') formed with 'به' + noun. 'گفتند' is the narrator's 'they said' (past third person plural). 'کای شهریار' is a vocative ('O, King'); 'کای' is the poetic vocative marker preceding the title.
segments.s534-g22: This is a short optative/wish: 'مبیناد' functions as a classical/pious form meaning 'may it not appear/let it not be seen' (here 'may not befall/appear'). 'چشمت' ('your eye') is the possessor‑suffixed noun, and 'بد روزگار' ('bad fate/bad days') is the object of that wish. Word order places the verb form before its nominal object for emphasis.
segments.s534-g23: 'چو' here means 'when' (classical narrative usage). 'مهرک بود' is 'Mehrak was', and 'دشمن اندر نهان' places the predicate 'enemy' followed by the locative phrase 'in/within hidden' ('an enemy hidden inside'). The clause sets a temporal/circumstantial background.
segments.s534-g24: 'چرا' is 'why'. 'جست' here carries the sense 'to seek/go (to exert oneself)' and combines with the modal 'باید' ('must/ought'), giving 'why must one go/search...'. 'به سختی جهان' ('into the hardship of the world') is a prepositional phrase with 'به' + noun; the whole asks why one should undertake worldly hardship given the hidden enemy.
segments.s534-g25: تو is the 2nd-person singular address (poetic 'thou') as subject; داری is the present-tense verb 'you have/possess.' بزرگی is thenoun predicate 'greatness.' The coordinate phrase گیهان تراست uses تراست (an archaic/poetic copular/possessive phrase) meaning 'is yours' so the clause reads 'you have greatness and the world is yours.'
segments.s534-g26: همه بندگانیم is a nominal predicate with the 1st‑person plural suffix -یم on بندگان implying 'we are all servants/subjects.' The conjunction و links the next clause فرمان تراست where فرمان is the noun 'command' and تراست again means 'is yours' (the command belongs to you).
segments.s534-g27: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a purpose or subordinate clause ('so that' / 'that'), and خوان بیاراستند is a past, 3rd‑person plural verb phrase 'they prepared/arrayed the feast' (بیاراستند = they adorned/arranged the table).
segments.s534-g28: This is a list of what was requested: می‌و جام = 'wine and cup(s)' (poetically run together here), followed by رامشگران خواستند where خواستند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they wanted/asked for' and رامشگران are those who bring delight (entertainers/comforters).
segments.s534-g29: به خوان بر نهادند is a verbal phrase: نهادند (past 3rd‑pl. of نهادن 'to place') with the preposition بر 'on' and the goal به خوان 'onto the table/banquet,' so 'they placed on the table.' چندی modifies بره: 'several/ some lamb(s)'.
segments.s534-g30: به خوردن نهادند uses نهادند 'they placed' with the verbal noun خوردن 'for eating' introduced by به — 'they put (it) down for eating.' سر یکسره is a noun + postmodifier: سر 'head' and یکسره 'entire/complete,' i.e. 'the whole head.'
segments.s534-g31: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' نان را has the object marker را on 'bread.' به خوردن گرفت is a common construction meaning 'took (it) for eating' (گرفت = took/seized). اردشیر at the end is the subject, placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s534-g32: همانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that same moment.' بیامد is past 'came.' یکی تیز تیر is a noun phrase 'one/ a sharp arrow' (یکی = one/a certain, تیز = sharp, تیر = arrow); poetic word order places the quantifier/adjective in this sequence.
segments.s534-g49: بران is a poetic adverb meaning 'thereupon' or 'on that (occasion)' and introduces the clause. موبدان نامدار اردشیر is a noun phrase in apposition: موبدان (mōbeds/priests) followed by the adjective نامدار 'renowned' and the proper name اردشیر; the modifiers follow the head in classical Persian, so it reads 'Ardashir, renowned among the mobeds.'
segments.s534-g50: نوشته here means 'the writing/inscription' (direct object) and is followed by همی خواند, where همی is a classical/perfective-progressive particle giving a continuous or iterative sense ('kept/was') and خواند is the past (3rd person) form 'he read/recited'. آن چوب تیر functions as a genitive/specifier for نوشته ('the inscription of/on that wooden arrow'); classical Persian often juxtaposes such modifiers without a linking clitic.
segments.s534-g51: ز ... تا combines two prepositional particles to mark the span 'from the fortress as far as/to him'. دو فرسنگ is the logical subject (a distance) and بود is the past copula 'was' agreeing with that measure phrase: 'From the fortress to him was two parasangs.' Word order places the locational span before the copula, typical in poetry.
segments.s534-g52: دل مهتران is a possessive construction: دل 'heart(s)' with مهتران (pl.) 'the chiefs/elders' specifying whose hearts. زان سخن 'because of/from that speech' gives the cause, and تنگ بود is a predicative adjective + copula meaning 'were constrained/tight' (i.e. troubled, anxious). Classical word order places the possessor after the possessed noun.
segments.s534-g53: همی is the iterative/progressive particle again ('kept/was'), هر کسی literally 'each person' (functioning as 'everyone' here) is the subject, and خواندند is the past plural 'they cried/recited' (note plural ending -ند). آفرین is an exclamation/acclamation ('praise'/'bravo') that is what they shouted; in classical Persian such interjections can follow the verb.
segments.s534-g54: ز دادار marks the source/authority 'from the Creator'. بر فر is 'upon the farr' (فَر = the king's divine glory) and شاه زمین is a title-like apposition 'the king of the earth'; together this phrase specifies the object of their acclamation: praise for the king's farr as given by the Creator and manifest on earth. Classical Persian freely stacks these prepositional and genitive relations.
segments.s534-g41: نوشته here functions as 'the writing' or 'inscription' (past-participle/nominal use) and introduces what follows. بران تیر is an old-style locative/identifying phrase meaning 'on that arrow' (بران = 'that/thereon' attached before تیر). بر پهلوی is a second locative phrase 'on the side/flank' specifying where the inscription is placed; classical Persian often stacks such prepositional phrases.
segments.s534-g42: که‌ای is a contracted vocative (که ای / ای) used to call someone: 'O'. شاه is the addressee 'king'. داننده‌گر is an emphatic epithet 'one who knows/perceives' (formed with -گر as an intensifier). بشنوی is the present-subjunctive/imperative 'hear' directed to the king; in classical Persian the verb follows the vocative phrase.
segments.s534-g43: چنین تیز تیر is a noun phrase with two modifiers before the noun: چنین 'thus/such' and تیز 'swift/keen', applying to تیر 'arrow'. آمد is the simple past 'came'. از بام دژ is a location phrase 'from the roof of the fortress' where the possessor (دژ) follows the place noun (بام).
segments.s534-g44: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because'). از بخت uses از in the sense 'by/from (fortune)' marking the cause; بخت 'fortune' is modified by کرمست, an archaic adjectival/predicative form (کرم + -ست 'is') meaning 'kind/generous'. آرام دژ means 'the fortress was calm/at peace' (adjective follows the noun in this poetic order).
segments.s534-g45: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. انداختیمی is a 1st‑person plural past tense ('we threw' / 'we had thrown') serving as the protasis (conditional clause). بر اردشیر marks the target 'at Ardashir' with the preposition بر.
segments.s534-g46: بروبر is an adverb meaning 'directly/straight on' or 'face-to-face'. گذر 'pass' is the place where something would be found. یافتی is 2nd‑person past/subjunctive 'you would have found' (result clause of the conditional). پر تیر is an adjectival phrase 'full of arrows' (پر before the noun means 'full (of)').
segments.s534-g47: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / it ought not'. که introduces the subordinate clause. چون او means 'like him'. یکی شهریار is an indefinite noun phrase 'somebody / a certain person (be) king' with یکی functioning as 'one / someone' before شهریار.
segments.s534-g48: کند is the 3rd‑person singular verb 'does / makes' (used here to state the undesired outcome 'be made'). پست کرم is a noun phrase 'base / low generosity' (پست qualifies کرم). اندرین روزگار is a temporal phrase 'in these days' (اندرین = 'in these'). Together the clause condemns making someone king by mean generosity in the present times.

sentences.s534-s17: A plump, clean lamb sat among them, into which the arrow was wholly embedded.
sentences.s534-s18: The great, wise war‑makers — at that time they had no lack of bread and their hands were free (i.e. they were not constrained by want).
sentences.s534-s19: They saw an inscription on that sharp arrow; it was read by the scribe among those great men.
sentences.s534-s20: From grief, each person drew blood from his heart; one drew the arrow out of the lamb.
sentences.s534-s1: In Jahrom there was a man of base birth whose name was Mehrak Nush‑zād.
sentences.s534-s2: When he learned of Ardashir's departure, he remained at that reservoir thereafter.
sentences.s534-s3: Because the battlefield there was narrow and cramped, the road for bringing stews was blocked.
sentences.s534-s4: He came from Jahrom to the king's iwan; from every side he brought (or mustered) a great host.
sentences.s534-s5: They plundered all his treasure, and to the army they gave many moon‑like beauties and crowns.
sentences.s534-s6: When the news came to King Ardashir, he became pensive at the edge of the reservoir.
sentences.s534-s7: He kept saying, 'Why did I build the house unfinished? Why did I wage war on strangers?'
sentences.s534-s8: He summoned the chiefs of his army before him, and from Mehrak he poured forth many words.
sentences.s534-s9: He said, "What do you see, O chiefs of the army, that our state has become so constrained?"
sentences.s534-s10: I have tasted much bitterness of fate; the hardship caused by Mehrak does not figure in my reckoning.
sentences.s534-s11: They said aloud, "O King, may your eye not behold ill fate."
sentences.s534-s12: When Mehrak was an enemy hidden within, why must one seek (or take to) the hardships of the world?
sentences.s534-s13: You possess greatness and the world is yours; we are all your servants and the command is yours.
sentences.s534-s14: He commanded that the table be prepared; they asked for wine, cups, and entertainers.
sentences.s534-s15: They placed several lambs on the table; they set the whole head down to be eaten.
sentences.s534-s16: When Ardashir took the bread to eat, at that very moment a sharp arrow came.
sentences.s534-s25: Thereupon Ardashir, renowned among the mobeds, read the inscription on that wooden arrow.
sentences.s534-s26: From the fortress to him was two parasangs; the chiefs' hearts were troubled by that speech.
sentences.s534-s27: All the while each person cried 'Afarin!' — praise to the king's farr granted by the Creator upon the earth.
sentences.s534-s21: On that arrow was written, on its side: "O king, discerning one, hear."
sentences.s534-s22: Thus a swift arrow came from the roof of the fortress, for by kind fortune the citadel was at peace.
sentences.s534-s23: If we had thrown (it) at Ardashir, you would have found the pass straight ahead full of arrows.
sentences.s534-s24: It ought not to be that, because of someone like him, a man is made king—what base generosity in these times!
